steps.title: Tie sounds to predator threat
steps.content: The researchers claim the caterpillar sounds are aimed primarily at predators, not at members of the same species. The strongest support would show that the sounds happen specifically during attacks and stop when the threat is gone. That pattern would connect the sounds to defense rather than ordinary communication.

steps.title: Reject sound-type details
steps.content: Choice B only says different species made different sounds, which does not identify the sounds' target. Choice C weakens the claim by saying vocalizations were not detectable by predatory birds. Choice D describes consistency of sounds during attacks but not their purpose. A threat-triggered pattern most directly supports predator-directed sound. The answer is A.
